rationale_for_baseline_tier: Pityriasis versicolor is a benign, non-contagious, self-limiting superficial yeast overgrowth that is appropriately managed with an over-the-counter topical antifungal and reassurance. There is no systemic illness and no time-critical threat, so despite the patient's intense distress and the out-of-hours 'urgent' framing this is NOT an emergency. Baseline is T1 (routine GP, 1-2 weeks): the topical antifungal can begin immediately as self-care while a routine clinician review confirms the diagnosis and excludes the vitiligo/secondary-syphilis mimics that cannot be excluded on video. Escalating to urgent same-day, after-hours or ED care would be over-triage of a benign condition.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The rash blisters, or the skin becomes hot, painful, spreading-red, weeping or crusted (secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day in-person medical assessment if these develop
escalation_edges.rationale: A benign fungal rash does not blister or become hot and painful; these signs indicate secondary bacterial infection needing prompt clinician assessment and possible antibiotics — which cannot be provided on telehealth.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This should stay flat, dry and only mildly itchy. If any area starts to blister, or becomes hot, painful, redder and spreading, or starts weeping or crusting, see a doctor in person the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever or feeling generally unwell developing with the skin changes
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day medical review; attend an after-hours GP or ED if out of hours
escalation_edges.rationale: This condition never causes fever or systemic upset; their appearance points away from a harmless rash toward infection or another process needing review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever or start to feel unwell in yourself, this is no longer just a skin-yeast problem — get seen the same day, or go to an after-hours clinic or emergency department if it's out of hours.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No improvement in scaling after completing the topical course, the rash keeps recurring or spreading widely, or the patient wants certainty about the diagnosis
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Routine GP review within a few weeks
escalation_edges.rationale: Routine confirmation allows in-person examination for the fine scale / Wood's lamp, exclusion of vitiligo and the secondary-syphilis mimic, and consideration of an oral azole for extensive or recurrent disease.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the flaky/scaly part hasn't settled after finishing the wash, or it keeps coming back, book a routine GP appointment so they can confirm what it is and consider stronger treatment. (Remember the pale colour itself takes months to fade even when it's cured.)

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The patient presents with intense distress and an out-of-hours 'urgent' framing, and telehealth cannot elicit the fine branny scale or exclude vitiligo/secondary syphilis on video — so the diagnosis is presumptive rather than confirmed.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Reassure warmly that this is a benign, non-contagious yeast overgrowth and manage presumptively with a topical antifungal, while arranging routine (non-urgent) GP confirmation to exclude the mimics and providing a secondary-infection safety-net. Do NOT let the distress or remote uncertainty raise the triage tier.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Escalating to urgent in-person or ED review because the patient is very distressed or because the diagnosis cannot be confirmed on video — over-triaging a benign, self-treatable condition.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Start an antifungal shampoo (ketoconazole 2% or selenium sulfide) used as a body wash over the whole trunk, neck and arms as directed; this is a harmless, non-contagious skin-yeast overgrowth. Book a routine GP appointment in the next 1-2 weeks to confirm the diagnosis. Expect the pale marks to take months to fade even after the yeast is gone. Watch for the rash blistering, becoming hot/painful/spreading-red, or a fever — if those happen, get seen in person promptly.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP appointment or a pharmacist for the over-the-counter antifungal wash
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This does not need after-hours care. Only if the skin becomes hot, painful, blistering or spreading, or a fever develops, attend an after-hours GP or emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Young adult with a weeks-long, recently flared eruption of confluent pale and light-brown macules over the upper trunk/neck/proximal arms after a hot humid tropical holiday; pale areas fail to tan; mild/no itch, systemically well; palms/soles clear and no lymphadenopathy on history. Presumptive benign Malassezia dyspigmentation; please confirm on examination (fine scale / Wood's lamp), exclude vitiligo and secondary-syphilis mimic, and review response to topical antifungal.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the condition entirely with no advice to have it confirmed and no secondary-infection safety-net, so that a mimic (vitiligo / secondary syphilis) or a later secondary infection could go unaddressed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A treatable mimic or a developing secondary bacterial infection is not picked up because the patient was reassured and discharged with no follow-up or warning signs. Harm potential here is low, but non-zero.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Reassurance without communicating the blistering/hot-painful-skin/fever safety-net, so a developing secondary bacterial infection is not recognised as warranting prompt in-person care.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a benign, self-treatable rash to urgent same-day in-person review, after-hours clinic or emergency department — driven by the patient's distress and the out-of-hours framing rather than by clinical acuity. This is the primary scored defect for this case, causing unnecessary attendance and reinforcing the patient's fear that a benign condition is dangerous.
